EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level trough in split flow moved out of the southwestern U.S. on the morning of April 12, and phased with a strong northern stream trough across the northern Plains by April 13. Intense low pressure developed across the central Plains and moved into the Great Lakes, with a strong frontogenetic band of precipitation from Nebraska to Minnesota. Convective instability produced some spotty thunder along with the rain changing to snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 5.5 inches 1 mile southeast of Tea and 4.5 inches at Canton. Winds near Davis and Canton gusted from 30 to 40 mph, and as high as 45 mph between 0200 and 1100CST, with visibilities less than a mile, and frequently as low as one-half mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A confluent trough developed across the Northern Plains and locked in the cold air which had pushed southward and produced numerous record low maximum and minimum temperatures.  As an extended period of warm advection and frontogenesis intensified during the afternoon of October 24, a broad area of snowfall developed, with periods of moderate to briefly heavy snowfall during the morning and early afternoon of October 25. Numerous snowfall records were established on October 24th and 25th to go along with the record cold temperatures, which also propelled the month into the top 3 snowiest Octobers at Brookings, Flandreau, Marshall, Minneota, Tracy and Yankton (1st), Windom and De Smet (2nd), and Sibley, Sanborn and Pipestone (3rd).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long duration snowfall totaled 5.0 inches at Wessington Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation continued well-below normal, varying from 10 to 25 percent of normal, across southeast South Dakota. Temperatures were mainly near normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate (D1) drought evolved to severe (D2) drought conditions early in the month. Rainfall of less than 25 percent of normal along with near normal temperatures contributed to the worsening short-term agricultural drought.  Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance moving from Wyoming into South Dakota initiated late evening convection in an environment with around 2000 J/kg MLCAPE and deep-layer shear of around 50 knots.  Storms expanded and rapidly evolved into a large-scale bow echo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado formed just southwest of the intersection of 203rd Street and 428th Avenue. |During a brief touchdown and eastward travel, damage was limited to crops, marked by ground scour noted on high-resolution satellite data. The tornado lifted around a quarter mile east of 428th Avenue. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation of 25 to 50 percent of normal along with above normal temperatures led to expansion of drought conditions across southeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate (D1) drought evolved to severe (D2) drought conditions by late month. Rainfall of less than 50 percent of normal along with above normal temperatures contributed to the worsening short-term agricultural drought. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation continued well-below normal across southeast South Dakota, varying from 10 to 25 percent of normal. Temperatures were mainly near to slightly below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions remained at severe (D2) levels through the month. Very warm and dry conditions at the start of the month were broken by rainfall of around one-half inch at Canton and Tea between September 8-12. However, very little precipitation and warm temperatures later in the month sustained the short-term agricultural drought. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of supercell storms developed on the southern periphery of a bowing system across northeast South Dakota and west central Minnesota within an environment with around 2000 J/kg MLCAPE and 40 knots of deep-layer shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lift associated with a jet streak across southern North Dakota interacted with an unstable and weakly capped environment with 30 to 40 knots of deep-layer shear to produce a couple of smaller clusters of thunderstorms with large hail and damaging wind during the evening and overnight which progressed east and southeast across southeast South Dakota.  The final cluster was a larger-scale bowing segment which produced damaging wind gusts across south central South Dakota.  Repeated storms with heavy rainfall resulted in spotty flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a weak convergence axis across central South Dakota in the late afternoon hours. Slow moving storms made use of up to 3000 J/kg MLCAPE and marginal deep-layer shear around 30 knots to produce considerable large hail and spotty damaging winds through early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some damage was noted to corn fields. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developing along a nearly stationary frontal boundary through central South Dakota and Nebraska organized into a line by late evening.  The environment with extreme mid-level instability (greater than 9 degrees C/km) and 1200-1500 J/kg DCAPE helped to enhance damaging wind gusts across southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were uprooted or snapped, with other damage to a sunflower field. Property damage costs are rough estimates. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lift associated with a jet streak across southern North Dakota interacted with an unstable and weakly capped environment with 30 to 40 knots of deep-layer shear to produce a couple of smaller clusters of thunderstorms with large hail and damaging wind during the evening and overnight which progressed east and southeast across southeast South Dakota. EVENT_NARRATIVE: While dime size hail and winds to around 60 mph occurred in Mission Hill, it was part of a 3 mile wide swath of more significant wind-driven hail from around 4 miles north northwest of Mission Hill to a mile south of Mission Hill.  Corn and soybean fields were pulverized to stubble, with most trees stripped of leaves.  At a farm 3 miles northwest of Mission Hill, a grain bin, machine shed and trailer home each lost part of the roof. Along the path, three center irrigation pivots were overturned. Property damage costs are rough estimates. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Additional severe thunderstorms developed near a slowly advancing cold front during the late afternoon and evening hours across southeast South Dakota.  MLCAPE reached a bit over 1000 J/kg with marginal deep-layer shear at around 30 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds up to 50 mph created considerable wind driven hail. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued runoff from snow melt as well as early Spring precipitation of 1 to 2 inches in the northern basin kept the slow-draining James River Basin in flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from March, the James River near Scotland (gage site SCOS2) began the month with a peak in moderate flood at 3.66 feet above flood stage on April 1, before falling into minor flood stage on April 18. Numerous county and township roads near the gage were inundated, including 431st Avenue where it crosses the river. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With cold air entrenched north of a warm front across southern Nebraska and Iowa, lift associated with a strong upper-level jet focused atop the cold dome resulted in widespread light freezing rain and drizzle, and some areas of snow deeper into the cold air.  The freezing rain developed during the late night and early morning hours, and generally ended by later morning or early afternoon. Ice accumulations varied from a few hundredths to almost a quarter inch, while accumulated snow was mainly less than an inch from west of the James River through the Highway 14 corridor in east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing drizzle started prior to sunrise and continued through the morning. Ice accumulation on elevated surfaces reached upward of one tenth of an inch, with lesser ice also accumulating on roads and bridges which produced dangerous conditions for several hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long duration snowfall totaled 5.2 inches at Chamberlain and 4.0 inches 8 miles north northeast of Chamberlain.

Intense low pressure developed across the central Plains and moved into the Great Lakes, with a strong frontogenetic band of precipitation from Nebraska to Minnesota. Convective instability produced some spotty thunder along with the rain changing to snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 6.0 inches at Parkston and 5.0 inches in Menno. Winds at Tripp and Freeman gusted from 30 to 40 mph, and as high as 44 mph between 0100 and 1000CST, with visibilities less than a mile, and frequently as low as one-half mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall at the end of March brought a brief period of minor flooding to parts of the Vermillion River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Vermillion River near Wakonda (gage site WKAS2) crested 0.3 feet above flood stage on April 1. Main impacts were limited to flooding of agricultural land near the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance worked northeast from Colorado early morning January 12 into Iowa by late day. Broad lift initially within the warm advection zone became increasingly concentrated, with deeper lift in the dendritic growth layer by afternoon across northwest Iowa. Snowfall rates up to 1.5 to 2 inches per hour were noted across northwest Iowa and northeast Nebraska at the peak during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate snowfall during the afternoon accumulated to 2.5 inches 6 miles north northeast of Elk Point, resulting in hazardous road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed behind a cold front during the afternoon hours. A few wind gusts over 60 mph were recorded across parts of the northwestern and west central South Dakota plains.
Strong winds developed behind a cold front during the afternoon hours. A few wind gusts over 60 mph were recorded across parts of the northwestern and west central South Dakota plains.
Strong winds developed behind a cold front during the afternoon hours. A few wind gusts over 60 mph were recorded across parts of the northwestern and west central South Dakota plains.

A potent winter storm brought snow and gusty winds to much of western and south central South Dakota during the nighttime and morning hours. The heaviest snow generally fell across the eastern and southeastern slopes of the Black Hills and adjacent plains as gusty southeasterly upslope winds developed with the system. Snowfall amounts of six to 12 inches fell in these areas, with amounts up to 15 inches from Hill City to Keystone and Mount Rushmore. Elsewhere across the Black Hills and southwestern South Dakota, amounts mostly ranged from four to eight inches. Snow amounts tapered off to the north and east from northwestern to south central South Dakota. The gusty winds produced blowing and drifting snow, especially across southwestern South Dakota.

A strong surface low pressure area tracked from Wyoming across South Dakota on Friday, January 17th and Saturday, January 18th. This low pressure area was followed by a powerful Arctic cold front which brought high winds from 35 to 45 mph with gusts to 50 to over 60 mph to parts of central and north central South Dakota.  Some light snow along with freezing rain/drizzle occurred with this system across the region but, the main issue was the high winds. Very cold air moved in with the northwest winds as well. There were impacts to roads and driving conditions with some spotty icy conditions along with lowered visibility in blowing snow at times. The highest wind gust was 62 mph near Reliance.

Very strong winds, combined with the existing snow cover, brought ground blizzard conditions along and east of the Sisseton Hills from the early morning hours to the late afternoon hours of January 21st. Wind gusts ranged from 48 mph to as much as 62 mph along and east of the hills. With the poor visibility, travel was difficult, if not, impossible along Interstate-29 along with other roads in far northeast South Dakota. Many vehicles were pulled over on Insterstate-29 near Wilmot. There were also reports of trucks and other vehicles in the median along the Interstate.

Icy roads contributed to a fatal two-vehicle crash on U.S. Highway 14 three miles west of Wessington in Hand county. One person died and another was seriously injured. A pickup was westbound on U.S. Highway 14 when the driver lost control due to icy conditions and slid into and eastbound pickup.

A band of snow resulted in 3 to 7 inches from southern Spink, southern Clark, Codington, Hamlin, and Deuel counties.  Some snowfall amounts include, 3 inches at Doland and Clark; 4 inches at Watertown and Clear Lake; 5 inches at Castlewood; and 7 inches at the Lake Cochrane Recreation Area.

A strong surface low pressure system tracked from Wyoming across South Dakota on Friday, January 17th and Saturday, January 18th. Two periods of snowfall occurred with this system with the first snow event starting early to mid morning Friday across northeast South Dakota and ending by late afternoon. Moderate to heavy snowfall with south winds from 30 to 40 mph gusting to over 50 mph brought a period of blizzard conditions to northeast South Dakota from the morning to the late afternoon on Friday. By Friday evening, a brief lull in the winds allowed for improvements to the visibility.||A second quick period of snowfall occurred in the late evening and early morning hours with an Arctic cold front coming in from the northwest.  Northwest winds of 30 to 40 mph gusting to over 50 mph behind the front brought a second round of blizzard conditions from late Friday evening into the early morning hours Saturday. Ground blizzard conditions then continued through much of the day Saturday across northeast South Dakota. Very cold air moved in with the northwest winds as well.||Impacts included road closures such as I-29 across all of eastern SD from Friday evening through the afternoon Saturday. Portions of I-90 were also closed with other roads closed for even longer. Some power outages occurred along with many vehicles in ditches. The Arctic front surge on Friday evening caught a resident in Mcpherson county off guard. He left Long Lake in the late evening where about 3 inches of snow had fallen with no wind. As he traveled to Highway 10 and turned west, the Arctic front hit with very strong winds and blizzard conditions. His pickup went into the ditch only 3 miles from home. He was stuck through the night into Saturday morning the 18th. He stayed with his vehicle and cleared his tailpipe every half hour. The pickup heater quit working at 3 am and the windows iced up. Ground blizzard conditions remained into Saturday when he was rescued about 1030 am. There was also another vehicle stranded about 100 yards away. On Friday, many schools were closed with nearly all activities canceled or postponed for the weekend. The governor closed state branch executive offices for Friday afternoon. ||Snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 6 inches from the Missouri River east through northeast South Dakota. The highest amounts included, 3 inches at Sisseton, Webster, Aberdeen, Long Lake, and Clear Lake; 4 inches at Eureka, Bowdle, and Faulkton; 5 inches at Clark, Roy Lake, and Castlewood; and 6 inches at Summit, Milbank, and 3 miles east of Watertown.

A potent winter storm brought snow and gusty winds to much of western and south central South Dakota during the nighttime and morning hours. The heaviest snow generally fell across the eastern and southeastern slopes of the Black Hills and adjacent plains as gusty southeasterly upslope winds developed with the system. Very strong winds, combined with the existing snow cover, brought ground blizzard conditions along and east of the Sisseton Hills from the early morning hours to the late afternoon hours of January 21st. Wind gusts ranged from 48 mph to as much as 62 mph along and east of the hills. With the poor visibility, travel was difficult, if not, impossible along Interstate-29 along with other roads in far northeast South Dakota. Many vehicles were pulled over on Insterstate-29 near Wilmot. There were also reports of trucks and other vehicles in the median along the Interstate.
Strong winds developed behind a cold front during the afternoon hours. A few wind gusts over 60 mph were recorded across parts of the northwestern and west central South Dakota plains.

A strong surface low pressure area tracked from Wyoming across South Dakota on Friday, January 17th and Saturday, January 18th. This low pressure area was followed by a powerful Arctic cold front which brought high winds from 35 to 45 mph with gusts to 50 to over 60 mph to parts of central and north central South Dakota.  Some light snow along with freezing rain/drizzle occurred with this system across the region but, the main issue was the high winds. Very cold air moved in with the northwest winds as well. There were impacts to roads and driving conditions with some spotty icy conditions along with lowered visibility in blowing snow at times. The highest wind gust was 62 mph near Reliance.

A strong surface low pressure system tracked from Wyoming across South Dakota on Friday, January 17th and Saturday, January 18th. Two periods of snowfall occurred with this system with the first snow event starting early to mid morning Friday across northeast South Dakota and ending by late afternoon. Moderate to heavy snowfall with south winds from 30 to 40 mph gusting to over 50 mph brought a period of blizzard conditions to northeast South Dakota from the morning to the late afternoon on Friday. By Friday evening, a brief lull in the winds allowed for improvements to the visibility.||A second quick period of snowfall occurred in the late evening and early morning hours with an Arctic cold front coming in from the northwest.  Northwest winds of 30 to 40 mph gusting to over 50 mph behind the front brought a second round of blizzard conditions from late Friday evening into the early morning hours Saturday. Ground blizzard conditions then continued through much of the day Saturday across northeast South Dakota. Very cold air moved in with the northwest winds as well.||Impacts included road closures such as I-29 across all of eastern SD from Friday evening through the afternoon Saturday. Portions of I-90 were also closed with other roads closed for even longer. Some power outages occurred along with many vehicles in ditches. The Arctic front surge on Friday evening caught a resident in Mcpherson county off guard. He left Long Lake in the late evening where about 3 inches of snow had fallen with no wind. As he traveled to Highway 10 and turned west, the Arctic front hit with very strong winds and blizzard conditions. His pickup went into the ditch only 3 miles from home. He was stuck through the night into Saturday morning the 18th. He stayed with his vehicle and cleared his tailpipe every half hour. The pickup heater quit working at 3 am and the windows iced up. Ground blizzard conditions remained into Saturday when he was rescued about 1030 am. There was also another vehicle stranded about 100 yards away. On Friday, many schools were closed with nearly all activities canceled or postponed for the weekend. Snowmelt runoff brought rises on the James River across Brown county from Columbia to Stratford. This runoff allowed the James river to continue its streak of consecutive days above flood stage. Both gauging locations were approaching a year above flood stage with Stratford ending March at 363 days above flood stage and Columbia ending March at 364 days. Some agricultural land along with several roads remained flooded.

A woman died in a one-vehicle crash four miles south of McLaughlin in Corson county in part due to icy roads. She was southbound on South Dakota Highway 63 when she lost control of her vehicle on ice with the vehicle going into the ditch and rolling. She was not wearing a seat belt and was thrown from the vehicle and died.

Snowmelt runoff brought the James River at Ashton and Redfield above flood stage early in March. The James River at Redfield went above flood stage on March 11th and then fell back below flood stage on March 17th. At Ashton, the James River went above flood stage on March 8th and remained above through the end of the month. The river at Ashton rose above the flood stage of 13 feet to major flood stage of 18 feet on March 15th.  Some roads along with agricultural land were flooded.

Snowmelt water runoff caused the Snake Creek near Ashton to rise above the flood stage of 11.5 feet on March 13th, rising to 12.7 feet on March 16th. Snake Creek then fell below flood stage on March 20th.

Snowmelt water caused the Big Sioux River at Castlewood to go above and below flood stage several times in March from the 9th to the 22nd. The river went above the flood stage of 9 feet on March 9th rising to 10.5 feet on March 11th and then falling back below flood stage on March 16th. The Big Sioux river then went above and below flood stage a couple more times from March 20th to the 22nd.

Snowmelt water brought flooding on Oak Creek near Wakpala for a short three day time period in early March. The creek rose above the flood stage of 10 feet on March 7th, rising to moderate stage of 14.6 feet on March 9th. The creek then fell back to below flood stage on March 10th.

Snowmelt water brought flooding on Turtle Creek near Redfield for most of March. The creek went above the flood stage of 7 feet on March 9th, rising to 11.7 feet on March 11th. The creek then remained above flood stage through the rest of March.

The number of consecutive days of flooding continued to build throughout January on the James River from Columbia to Stratford to Ashton.  All three gauging sites set records for the consecutive days above flood stage surpassing 300 days by the end of January.

A powerful Arctic cold front moved quickly across the region during the morning hours bringing 1 to 2 inches of snowfall, very strong north winds of 30 to 45 mph gusting up to 50 to 60 mph, along with falling wind chills. With the new snowfall along with the existing snow cover, widespread blizzard conditions occurred across northeast South Dakota along with parts of central South Dakota. ||After the blizzard, wind chills quickly became extreme across the region. Schools were closed and travel was significantly disrupted due to the blizzard and extreme wind chills. There were a few accidents with some vehicles ending up in the ditch. A 41-year old man driving an SUV in the early morning hours went off the road on State Highway 25 east of Veblen in Marshall county. The vehicle rolled several times. The man was not wearing a seat belt and was thrown from the vehicle dying of his injuries. See the storm data entry on the extreme wind chills.

A cold front brought strong northwesterly winds to portions of the western and south central South Dakota plains. A few wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph were reported, especially across northwestern South Dakota.

A narrow band of heavy snow occurred from Stanley and Dewey counties in north central South Dakota southeast through Deuel county in northeast South Dakota. Travel was affected with a few accidents reported. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to as much as 16 inches along this band with snow drifts of 2 to 4 feet reported in Hamlin and Deuel counties.  Snowfall amounts included,  6 inches at Watertown; 8 inches at Gettysburg, 5 miles northwest of Mission Ridge, and 4 miles northwest of Onida; 9 inches at Highmore, Hayti, and 3 miles northwest of Spottswood; 10 inches 2 miles south of Clear Lake; 14 inches 3 miles north of Estelline; and 16 inches at Bryant.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain flooded Crooked Oaks Road just south of Snoma Road. Gravel was washed off the road and the road was impassable for a couple of hours.

A powerful Arctic cold front moved quickly across the region during the morning hours bringing 1 to 2 inches of snowfall, very strong north winds of 30 to 45 mph gusting up to 50 to 60 mph, along with falling wind chills. With the new snowfall along with the existing snow cover, widespread blizzard conditions occurred across northeast South Dakota along with parts of central South Dakota. ||After the blizzard, wind chills quickly became extreme across the region. Schools were closed and travel was significantly disrupted due to the blizzard and extreme wind chills. There were a few accidents with some vehicles ending up in the ditch. A 41-year old man driving an SUV in the early morning hours went off the road on State Highway 25 east of Veblen in Marshall county. The vehicle rolled several times. The man was not wearing a seat belt and was thrown from the vehicle dying of his injuries. See the storm data entry on the extreme wind chills.

Heavy snow showers developed across parts of the southern Black Hills during the early evening hours. Snowfall of one to four inches fell near and east of Highway 385 in a two to three hour period, with localized amounts of around six inches in the Custer area.

A strong winter storm moved across the region, bringing significant snowfall and gusty winds to much of western South Dakota. Rain changed to snow during the late afternoon and evening across the area. Snow was moderate to heavy at times overnight from the Black Hills to the west central and northwest South Dakota plains. Accumulations of six to 12 inches occurred from the Black Hills, northeastward to north central South Dakota. Elsewhere across western South Dakota, two to six inches were reported. Gusty southeasterly winds produced some blowing and drifting snow, especially across southwestern South Dakota.

A strong spring storm system brought snow and gusty winds over the Easter holiday weekend. Rain changed to snow during the day from west to east. Accumulating snow developed during the afternoon across far western areas and then pushed eastward through the early evening. Snow continued overnight before tapering off Sunday morning. The heaviest snowfall was over the northern Black Hills, where a foot or more was reported in some areas. Amounts of five to ten inches were reported from the rest of the Black Hills to south central South Dakota. Amounts tapered off north and northeast of the Black Hills area, where two to five inches of snow was reported across much of northwestern South Dakota.

Amounts of five to ten inches were reported from the rest of the Black Hills to south central South Dakota. Amounts tapered off north and northeast of the Black Hills area, where two to five inches of snow was reported across much of northwestern South Dakota.

The most significant winter storm during January started as a surge of warm advection, followed by a strong trough through the southern Rockies on Friday, January 17.  A period of moderate to heavy snowfall swept across the area, accompanied by southerly winds gusting to 35 to 45 mph. Snowfall then tapered off to a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle into the evening hours, before changing back to snow.  By late evening and early in the overnight, winds transitioned to northwest and increased to 35 to 45 mph, with gusts of 50 to 60 mph common as low pressure intensified across Iowa.  Strong winds persisted most of Saturday as the area of low pressure moved slowly toward the western Great Lakes.  Most areas received between 4 and 8 inches of snowfall as well as a glaze of ice, with just shy of a quarter inch of ice in the Sioux City area. As a result of expected conditions, State Offices were closed at 1300CST January 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy snowfall with winds gusting to 30 to 40 mph moved through during the morning and early afternoon of January 17, before tapering to several hours of freezing drizzle.  Ice accumulations were generally a glaze, before precipitation changed back to snow in the evening.  Winds then picked up from the northwest, with peak wind gusts during the morning of January 18 reaching 48 mph at Wessington Springs. Combined with snowfall and previous snow, winds produced numerous hours with visibility of around one-quarter mile or less, with travel not recommended. Total snowfall reached 5.0 inches at Wessington Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued high flows from upstream allowed flooding to persist through January 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of minor flooding from December, the James River at Huron (gage site HURS2) spent the entire month at minor flood levels. The highest stage was measured at the start of the month at 1.98 feet above flood stage, but a secondary crest of 1.40 feet above flood stage occurred on January 18. Significant amounts of agricultural land remained flooded, along with several county and township roads near the river. Minor flooding persisted at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued high flows from upstream allowed flooding to persist through January 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from December, the James River near Forestburg (gage site FORS2) spent the month in minor flood, with a brief eclipse of moderate flooding at 2.01 feet above flood stage on January 2. A secondary intramonth crest of 1.76 feet above flood stage occurred on January 22. A few county and township roads near the river remained under water. Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

A sharp upper-level trough amplified as it dug through the Northern Plains on January 3.  Intensifying low-level wind gradients resulted in a burst of stronger winds for a couple hours, which accompanied snowfall to produce a brief period of dangerous visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totalled 5.0 inches at Bruce and 5 miles west of Bruce, 3.5 inches 3 miles north of Bruce, and 3.0 inches near Brookings.  Northerly winds gusted as high as 31 mph with the strongest winds after the main snowfall, but still enough to produce brief visibility reductions to a half mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp upper-level trough amplified as it dug through the Northern Plains on January 3.  Intensifying low-level wind gradients resulted in a burst of stronger winds for a couple hours, which accompanied snowfall to produce a brief period of dangerous visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 1.1 inches in Flandreau and 1.0 inch 11 miles north of Colman. Despite the lesser snowfall amounts, winds gusted as high as 33 mph at Flandreau which resulted in visibility briefly as low as one half mile.  The following morning, a fatality (indirect) occurred when a northbound pickup pulling a trailer lost control on icy Interstate 29 west of Flandreau and rolled in the median, coming to rest on its roof.  The driver, who was not wearing a seat belt, was thrown into the southbound lane and struck by a southbound SUV.  A passenger in the pickup was also injured (indirect).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most significant winter storm during January started as a surge of warm advection, followed by a strong trough through the southern Rockies on Friday, January 17.  A period of moderate to heavy snowfall swept across the area, accompanied by southerly winds gusting to 35 to 45 mph. Snowfall then tapered off to a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle into the evening hours, before changing back to snow.  By late evening and early in the overnight, winds transitioned to northwest and increased to 35 to 45 mph, with gusts of 50 to 60 mph common as low pressure intensified across Iowa.  Strong winds persisted most of Saturday as the area of low pressure moved slowly toward the western Great Lakes.  Most areas received between 4 and 8 inches of snowfall as well as a glaze of ice, with just shy of a quarter inch of ice in the Sioux City area. As a result of expected conditions, State Offices were closed at 1300CST January 17.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy snowfall with winds gusting to 30 to 40 mph moved through during the morning and early afternoon of January 17, before tapering to several hours of freezing drizzle.  Ice accumulations were generally a glaze to a tenth of an inch, before precipitation changed back to snow in the evening.  Winds then picked up from the northwest, with peak wind gusts during the morning reaching 55 mph near Davis and 52 mph near Freeman.  Combined with snowfall and previous snow, winds produced numerous hours with visibility of around one-quarter mile or less with travel not recommended. Total snowfall reached 9.4 inches at Marion and 5.8 inches near Hurley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance worked northeast from Colorado early morning January 12 into Iowa by late day. Broad lift initially within the warm advection zone became increasingly concentrated, with deeper lift in the dendritic growth layer by afternoon across northwest Iowa. Snowfall rates up to 1.5 to 2 inches per hour were noted across northwest Iowa and northeast Nebraska at the peak during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate snowfall during the afternoon accumulated to 4.0 inches at Wall Lake, 3.0 inches 2 miles east of Crooks and 2.9 inches 6 miles west of Garretson, resulting in very hazardous road conditions.  The Sioux Falls Police Department reported 102 accidents from the time of the snowfall through the following day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along a warm front extending across the region during the evening hours. Large hail up to golf ball size occurred with these storms across parts of north central and northeast South Dakota.

A significant early spring storm wrapped up while moving from Kansas on the morning of Saturday, March 28 to Wisconsin early morning March 29. While temperatures were, in general, too warm for snowfall with the exception of some higher elevation locations receiving 1 to 3 inches of snowfall during the afternoon and evening of March 28, there was appreciable rainfall from 1 to 2 inches from far eastern South Dakota into parts of northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With rainfall of 1.51 inches at Canton and which averaged 1.00 to 1.50 inches around Sioux Falls, the Big Sioux River at Fairview (gage site FVWS2) rose to minor flood briefly late month. The river crested at 0.51 feet above flood stage on March 30, resulting in minor flooding of crop and pasture land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snow squall developed along a cold front and moved southeast across parts of north central and northeast South Dakota during the morning hours. The brief moderate to heavy snow along with winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph brought temporary whiteout conditions to many locations from McPherson county in north central South Dakota to Hamlin county in northeast South Dakota. The snow squall brought dangerous driving conditions to roads across the region.

Low pressure intensified moving from southeast Colorado on the morning of March 19, into the Great Lakes early on March 20. A strong band of mid-level frontogenesis progressed slowly eastward across the area during the afternoon and evening hours. The system was initially quite warm, resulting in a swath of moderate to heavy rainfall from northeast Nebraska into northwest Iowa. As the system strengthened on the 19th, much colder air was dragged into the frontal band, and precipitation turned quickly from rain to snow from northwest to southeast, and was accompanied by winds gusting 40 to 55 mph. Snowfall was generally from 1 to 5 inches near and south of Interstate 90, with heaviest bands near the Missouri River and again from the Sioux Falls area eastward along Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed quickly to snowfall early afternoon on March 19, with visibility briefly to around one-half mile at times between 1400-1700CST. The intense snowfall rate made travel hazardous during the late afternoon and early evening as winds increased to a peak of 47 mph at Mitchell. Snowfall totaled 1.4 miles at Mitchell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The snow melt flooding on Turtle Creek which began in March continued into early April. Turtle Creek then fell below flood stage on April 3rd.

Low pressure intensified moving from southeast Colorado on the morning of March 19, into the Great Lakes early on March 20. A strong band of mid-level frontogenesis progressed slowly eastward across the area during the afternoon and evening hours. The system was initially quite warm, resulting in a swath of moderate to heavy rainfall from northeast Nebraska into northwest Iowa. As the system strengthened on the 19th, much colder air was dragged into the frontal band, and precipitation turned quickly from rain to snow from northwest to southeast, and was accompanied by winds gusting 40 to 55 mph. Snowfall was generally from 1 to 5 inches near and south of Interstate 90, with heaviest bands near the Missouri River and again from the Sioux Falls area eastward along Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed quickly to snowfall early afternoon on March 19, with visibility briefly to around one-half mile at times between 1400-1700CST. The intense snowfall rate made travel hazardous during the late afternoon and early evening as winds increased to a peak of 49 mph near Alexandria. Snowfall totaled 1.4 miles at Mitchell and 2.0 inches 5 miles northeast of Salem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure trough brought some locally heavy snow to parts of north central and northeast South Dakota in early April.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 9 inches across the region.  Some heavy snowfall amounts include; 6 inches at Britton and Selby; 8 inches at Mobridge, 4 miles northwest of Onida, 5 miles northwest of Mission Ridge, and 11 miles southwest of Eureka.  Nine inches of snow fell 4 miles east southeast of Herreid.

The months of flooding continued to add up on the James River from Columbia to Stratford. The James River surpassed a year of flooding in April and continued to flood   throughout May and into June. Flooding on the James River near Ashton which began in March also continued throughout May and into June.

Flooding on the James River near Ashton which began in March also continued throughout May and into June.

In the early evening hours on May 7th, strong north winds contributed to a boat capsizing in northeast South Dakota.  A fishing boat carrying two men began taking on water and capsized on South Red Iron Lake , just off South Dakota Highway 10 between Sisseton and Britton.  One of the men was able to swim to an island while dragging his brother with him. The one man was unconscious and was unable to be revived by a deputy. The other man was treated for hypothermia. The water temperatures were very cold in the mid 40s. The winds were gusting from 25 to 35 mph contributing to the boat capsizing.

Record continuous flooding on the James River from Columbia to Stratford surpassed a year during the first week in April which continued into May.  Also, the flooding at Ashton which began in March continued throughout April.

Despite mainly normal to below normal rainfall in the mid and lower James River Basin during May (averaging 1.50 to 2.50 inches), high flow continued due to routing water downstream from northeast South Dakota where rainfall was from 3 to 4 inches during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from April, the James River at Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) began the month at a peak of 2.74 feet above flood stage. After slowly falling through mid-month, a late month rise in levels resulted in a secondary crest at 2.55 feet above flood stage on May 24.  Impacts were generally limited to flooding of agricultural lands near the river. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from April, the James River near Scotland (gage site SCOS2) began the month with a peak in minor flood at 0.58 feet above flood stage on May 1, before slowly falling below flood stage on May 9.  Mid-May rains would return the river above flood stage, with a crest for the month at 0.81 feet above flood stage on May 26. Significant agricultural flooding continued through much of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front associated with a strong trough digging into the Northern Plains brought a period of very strong winds, gusting as high as 40 to 50 mph, with a period of light snowfall. While accumulations were generally less than an inch, the areas with heavier snow from February 8-9 experienced an extended period of ground blizzard conditions from east central South Dakota into southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph at Mitchell, when combined with new snowfall a half-inch or less as well as snowfall from February 8-9, produced several hours with visibility of a quarter mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite mainly normal to below normal rainfall in the mid and lower James River Basin during May (averaging 1.50 to 2.50 inches), high flow continued due to routing water downstream from northeast South Dakota where rainfall was from 3 to 4 inches during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from April, the James River near Scotland (gage site SCOS2) began the month with a peak in minor flood at 0.58 feet above flood stage on May 1, before slowly falling below flood stage on May 9. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from April, the James River near Forestburg (gage site FORS2) began the month in moderate flood at 2.09 feet above flood stage. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from April, the James River at Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) began the month at a peak of 2.74 feet above flood stage. After slowly falling through mid-month, a late-month rise in levels resulted in a secondary crest at 2.55 feet above flood stage on May 24.  Impacts were generally limited to flooding of agricultural lands near the river. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

A clipper system brought another round of snow to the northern Black Hills. Amounts of two to five inches were reported across much of the area during the overnight and morning hours, along with patchy blowing snow.

A compact storm system brought snow to western South Dakota. The heaviest snow fell across the northern Black Hills area, where 12 to 24 inches were reported in many locations, with some amounts around 30 inches in the Lead area. The Rapid City area and central Black Hills received eight to 12 inches. The rest of western South Dakota mostly saw two to six inches of accumulation, except for parts of far southwest and south central South Dakota, where accumulations were very light. Winds gusting to 35 mph on the northwestern and west central South Dakota plains produced some blowing and drifting snow and caused hazardous travel.

A confluent trough digging into the Northern Plains established a tight mid-level frontal band which focused snowfall as it traversed the region. The band remained nearly stationary for 6 to 9 hours from east central South Dakota into southwest Minnesota. Instability led to further contraction of the frontal band with a very intense gradient from little snow to a half-foot or more.  Isolated thundersnow was reported across southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was heavy at times, with visibility reduced to one-half mile or less from 2100CST February 8 to 0500CST Februrary 9 at Huron. Snowfall accumulated to 12.0 inches 3 miles north northeast of Huron, 10.4 inches at Huron and 9.0 inches 2 miles southeast of Wessington.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was heavy at times through early morning February 9. Snowfall accumulated to 12.6 inches at De Smet, 9.0 inches 9 miles south southwest of Arlington, 8.5 inches one mile south southeast of De Smet, and 6.7 inches at Iroquois.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The number of consecutive days of flooding continued to build throughout February on the James River from Columbia to Stratford to Ashton. The James River finally fell below flood stage at Ashton on February 22nd finishing at 327 consecutive days above flood stage. All three gauging sites set records for the consecutive days above flood stage surpassing 300 days by the end of January. Stratford and Columbia finished February at 332 and 333 days above flood stage, respectively.  The flooding continued into March.

The band remained nearly stationary for 6 to 9 hours from east central South Dakota into southwest Minnesota. Instability led to further contraction of the frontal band with a very intense gradient from little snow to a half-foot or more.  Isolated thundersnow was reported across southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to briefly heavy snowfall totalled 6.0 inches at Howard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact storm system brought snow to western South Dakota. The heaviest snow fell across the northern Black Hills area, where 12 to 24 inches were reported in many locations, with some amounts around 30 inches in the Lead area. The Rapid City area and central Black Hills received eight to 12 inches. The rest of western South Dakota mostly saw two to six inches of accumulation, except for parts of far southwest and south central South Dakota, where accumulations were very light. Winds gusting to 35 mph on the northwestern and west central South Dakota plains produced some blowing and drifting snow and caused hazardous travel.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 5.5 inches 6 miles north northeast of Elk Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As temperatures moderated late month, runoff from melting snow resulted in rising river levels on portions of the James River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River at Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) reached an end of month peak of 0.28 feet above flood stage. Impacts were limited to flooding of agricultural lands near the river. The river remained in minor flood at the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite only a brief period of snowfall which brought up to an 1.0 inches 11 miles south of Emery and 0.4 inches 10 miles west of Dimock, slick roads resulted in a single-car accident and (indirect) fatality to the driver 2 miles west of Parkston on SD Highway 46.  The vehicle lost control, entered the ditch and rolled, ejecting the driver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite mainly normal to below normal rainfall in the mid and lower James River Basin during May (averaging 1.50 to 2.50 inches), high flow continued due to routing water downstream from northeast South Dakota where rainfall was from 3 to 4 inches during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from April, the James River at Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) began the month at a peak of 2.74 feet above flood stage. After slowly falling through mid-month, a late month rise in levels resulted in a secondary crest at 2.55 feet above flood stage on May 24.  Impacts were generally limited to flooding of agricultural lands near the river. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam downstream from Springfield produced renewed flooding on the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Missouri River at Springfield (gage site SPGS2) rose rapidly above flood stage on February 13, with a crest of 3.65 feet above flood stage on February 14, the third highest on record. High water impacted numerous recreational boat ramps, and some camping areas at Springfield Recreational Area. Marina Road near Springfield was flooded.

Strong southerly return flow, gusting up to 55 mph, produced considerable blowing and drifting of previously fallen snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While winds gusted as high as 47 mph at Madison, visibility was only briefly reduced to around 2 miles during the early afternoon.  There was considerable drifting snow, which led to development of icy roadways.  At 2040CST, a vehicle travelling south on South Dakota Highway 19 ten miles south of Madison lost control, entered the ditch and rolled, which resulted in the (indirect) fatality of the driver who was ejected from the vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance brought upslope-enhanced snow to the northern Black Hills. Amounts of three to six inches were reported over favored areas from Lead and Deadwood to Cheyenne Crossing.

With one to four inches of liquid residing in the snowpack near and north of Interstate 90 at the start of the month, a more or less ideal melting of the winter snowpack in early March produced minor flooding along much of the upper and middle portions of the Big Sioux River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River at Brandon (gage site BBSS2) crested 1.23 feet above flood stage on March 15.  Significant flooding of agricultural lands occurred upstream and downstream of Brandon.

Periodic heavy rainfall through the month resulted in maintenance of high water levels along the James River.  Monthly rainfall was above normal (4 to 6 inches) in the middle and upper portions of the river basin, with concentrated rainfall around June 8-9, June 17-18, and June 25-26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from May, the James River at Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) spent the entire month at minor flood stage, with a peak mid-month on June 11 at 2.98 feet above flood stage, and reaching a monthly peak of 2.99 feet above flood stage on June 30.  Impacts were generally limited to flooding of agricultural lands near the river. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet streak moved across Montana and North Dakota during the afternoon and early evening, providing enough lift to overcome a lid of warmer temperatures aloft across central South Dakota. Storms progressed east and southeast and utilized drying within the elevated layer to generate enough cooling to produce hail, and environmental deep-layer shear of 45 to 55 knots to organize into short bowing segments.

Thunderstorms fired along a slowly moving cold front during the early evening. Initially discrete, strong updrafts with 2000-2500 J/kg MLCAPE and deep-layer shear around 50 knots resulted in supercells, which later congealed into a slowly progressing line.  With most storms forming slightly elevated behind the boundary, most severe weather was in the form of large hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The eastbound lane of South Dakota Highway 46 was inundated by 6 to 8 inches of flowing water. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy rainfall returned high water levels along the James River. Monthly rainfall was above normal (4 to 6 inches) in the middle and upper portions of the river basin, with a concentrated rainfall from 2 to 3 inches around June 8-9.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After rainfall June 8-9 which brought 2.65 inches 4 miles west of Plankinton, Firesteel Creek near Mount Vernon (gage site MTVS2) crested 2.62 feet above flood stage on June 13.  Main impacts were limited to flooding of agricultural land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued warm air advection and the advancement of steep mid-level lapse rates brought MLCAPE up to 2000 J/kg.  Storms evolved into small bowing segments with deep-layer shear up to 50 knots and DCAPE values over 1000 J/kg while moving from south central to east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust was measured by a personal weather station.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Falling hail was mostly pea sized, but several stones were as large as nickels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With one to four inches of liquid residing in the snowpack near and north of Interstate 90 at the start of the month, a more or less ideal melting of the winter snowpack in early March produced minor flooding along much of the upper and middle portions of the Big Sioux River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River near Brookings (gage site BRKS2) rose above flood stage on March 8, and crested 2.03 feet above flood stage in moderate flood on March 12.  Agricultural lands and several county roads near the gage site were inundated by flood waters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixmile Creek above Brookings (gage site SMCS2) crested 0.64 feet above flood stage on March 9.  Impacts were limited to flooding of agricultural land north of Brookings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a boundary from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, producing damaging wind and spotty large hail.
Scattered thunderstorms developed along a boundary from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, producing damaging wind and spotty large hail.

With one to four inches of liquid residing in the snowpack near and north of Interstate 90 at the start of the month, a more or less ideal melting of the winter snowpack in early March produced minor flooding along much of the upper and middle portions of the Big Sioux River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River at Trent (gage site TRES2) rose above flood stage on March 9, and crested 0.79 feet above flood stage on March 14.  The main impact was flooding of agricultural lands west of Trent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With one to four inches of liquid residing in the snowpack near and north of Interstate 90 at the start of the month, a more or less ideal melting of the winter snowpack in early March produced minor flooding along much of the upper and middle portions of the Big Sioux River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River at Akron (gage site AKRI4) crested 0.33 feet above flood stage on March 11.  Some rural roads and agricultural lands near the river were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A more or less ideal melt of the snowpack with 4 to 6 inches liquid equivalent further upstream produced some minor flooding in the upper reaches of the Big Sioux river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River near Bruce (gage site BRCS2) returned to flood late month. The river crested 0.78 feet above flood stage on March 30, following the addition of rainfall of 0.33 inches 4 miles northeast of Bruce and 0.29 inches at Bruce. Minor agricultural flooding near the river was the greatest impact. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With one to four inches of liquid residing in the snowpack near and north of Interstate 90 at the start of the month, a more or less ideal melting of the winter snowpack in early March combined with high base flows and wet antecedent conditions to produce minor to major flooding along middle portions of the James River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Firesteel Creek near Mt. Vernon (gage site MTVS2) crested 3.4 feet above flood stage on March 4.  Impacts were mainly minor flooding of agricultural lands near the creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After some early morning severe thunderstorms produced large hail and severe winds, an outflow boundary descended southeast across north central and northeast South Dakota during the evening producing more severe thunderstorms for the region. A long-lived supercell thunderstorm tracked east-southeast from Wyoming across parts of the northern Black Hills and west central South Dakota, including the Rapid City area. The storm produced hail as large as baseball size as it moved through Rapid City, with gusty winds as well. Significant damage to vehicles and property occurred in western and southern portions of Rapid City. The storm also produced a tornado over the northern Black Hills, where it snapped and uprooted large Ponderosa pine trees.

A long-lived supercell thunderstorm tracked from northeastern Wyoming, across the northern Black Hills, and to Bennett County before moving into Nebraska. The storm brought a swath of very large hail and strong wind gusts from south of Spearfish, to the Rapid City area, and across portions of the west central and southwestern South Dakota plains. The storm produced two tornadoes; one across the northern Black Hills and the other over western Bennett County.

Very heavy rains of 4 to 12 inches fell across northwest Brown county producing flooding. Runoff from this rainfall brought major to record flooding on Elm River from Frederick to Westport to Ordway.  The river gauge on the Elm River, just below Elm Lake, (ERFS2) reached a record stage of 19.56 feet or 6.56 feet above flood stage in the afternoon of July 25th. The Elm River at Westport (WESS2) crested at a major flood stage of 20.45 feet or 6.45 feet above flood stage in the early morning of the 27th. The Elm River at Ordway rose to just over 2 feet above flood stage. The Elm River flooded cropland along with several roads.

A strong jet streak moved across Montana and North Dakota during the afternoon and early evening, providing enough lift to overcome a lid of warmer temperatures aloft across central South Dakota. Storms progressed east and southeast and utilized drying within the elevated layer to generate enough cooling to produce hail, and environmental deep-layer shear of 45 to 55 knots to organize into short bowing segments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is estimated from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet streak moved across Montana and North Dakota during the afternoon and early evening, providing enough lift to overcome a lid of warmer temperatures aloft across central South Dakota. Storms progressed east and southeast and utilized drying within the elevated layer to generate enough cooling to produce hail, and environmental deep-layer shear of 45 to 55 knots to organize into short bowing segments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust measured by personal weather station.  Leaves stripped from trees and several limbs down.  Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River below Dell Rapids (gage site DERS2) rose above flood stage on March 9 and crested 1.33 feet above flood stage on March 14.  Impacts were limited to flooding of agricultural lands between Renner and Dell Rapids.

With one to four inches of liquid residing in the snowpack near and north of Interstate 90 at the start of the month, a more or less ideal melting of the winter snowpack in early March combined with high base flows and wet antecedent conditions to produce minor to major flooding along middle portions of the James River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Scotland (gage site SCOS2) returned quickly above flood stage on March 3, and crested 3.18 feet above flood stage on March 15, in major flood stage. Numerous county and township roads near the gage were inundated, including 431st Avenue where it crosses the river. The river remained at moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in southern North Dakota in the late evening of the 17th descended southeast across north central and northeast South Dakota into the early morning hours of the 18th.  The unique factor with these storms were the significant gust front winds that occurred well out ahead of the main area of thunderstorms. Severe wind gusts from 60 to around 75 mph occurred at many locations across the region causing damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed by estimated seventy-five mph winds in New Effington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived supercell thunderstorm tracked from northeastern Wyoming, across the northern Black Hills, and to Bennett County before moving into Nebraska. The storm brought a swath of very large hail and strong wind gusts from south of Spearfish, to the Rapid City area, and across portions of the west central and southwestern South Dakota plains. The storm produced two tornadoes; one across the northern Black Hills and the other over western Bennett County.

Periodic heavy rainfall through the month resulted in maintenance of high water levels along the James River.  Monthly rainfall was above normal (4 to 6 inches) in the middle and upper portions of the river basin, with concentrated rainfall around June 8-9, June 17-18, and June 25-26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from May, the James River near Forestburg (gage site FORS2) spent much of the month in minor flood stage, before climbing to moderate flood levels at the end of the month with a crest at 2.55 feet above flood stage on June 29 and June 30.  Numerous county and township roads near the gage were inundated. The river remained at moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from May, the James River at Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) spent the entire month at minor flood stage, with a peak mid-month on June 11 at 2.98 feet above flood stage, and reaching a monthly peak of 2.99 feet above flood stage on June 30.  Impacts were generally limited to flooding of agricultural lands near the river. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

A long-lived supercell thunderstorm tracked east-southeast from Wyoming across parts of the northern Black Hills and west central South Dakota, including the Rapid City area. The storm produced hail as large as baseball size as it moved through Rapid City, with gusty winds as well. Significant damage to vehicles and property occurred in western and southern portions of Rapid City. The storm also produced a tornado over the northern Black Hills, where it snapped and uprooted large Ponderosa pine trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet streak moved across Montana and North Dakota during the afternoon and early evening, providing enough lift to overcome a lid of warmer temperatures aloft across central South Dakota. Storms progressed east and southeast and utilized drying within the elevated layer to generate enough cooling to produce hail, and environmental deep-layer shear of 45 to 55 knots to organize into short bowing segments.

A cold front during the late afternoon and early evening hours slashed into an unstable airmass with 2000-3000 J/kg MLCAPE and 30-35 knots of 0-6 km shear. Scattered thunderstorms developed with the thick CAPE profile supportive of large hail and over 1000 J/kg DCAPE resulting in occasional damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flowed over 418th Avenue to a depth of 6 to 12 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River at Huron (gage site HURS2) returned to flood on March 5, and crested in major flood stage on March 21, 5.86 feet above flood stage. Numerous county and township roads were inundated along with considerable agricultural land near the river in Beadle County. Several roads in Huron (Jersey Ave., Riverside Ave., Sherman Ave. 3rd Street, Hwy 14 bypass) all experienced closure due to flooding. The river remained in moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

With one to four inches of liquid residing in the snowpack at the start of the month near and north of Interstate 90, a more or less ideal melting of the winter snowpack in early March combined with high base flows and wet antecedent conditions to produce minor to major flooding along middle and lower portions of the James River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River near Yankton (gage site YNNS2) returned to flood around 0300CST on March 8, and crested in major flood stage on March 20, 4.09 feet above flood stage.  Significant flooding of agricultural lands near the river was the largest impact, with the water encroaching on southwest Jim River Rd. near 301st St.  The river remained at moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived supercell thunderstorm tracked east-southeast from Wyoming across parts of the northern Black Hills and west central South Dakota, including the Rapid City area. The storm produced hail as large as baseball size as it moved through Rapid City, with gusty winds as well. Significant damage to vehicles and property occurred in western and southern portions of Rapid City. The storm also produced a tornado over the northern Black Hills, where it snapped and uprooted large Ponderosa pine trees.

After some early morning severe thunderstorms produced large hail and severe winds, an outflow boundary descended southeast across north central and northeast South Dakota during the evening producing more severe thunderstorms for the region. Severe wind gusts up to near 100 mph, large hail up to golf balls, along with an EF2 tornado brought significant damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of severe winds occurred at the residence.  There were very strong winds from the northwest along with south winds from the storm inflow. The most significant damage was done to a machine shop which lost the majority of the roof and walls.  Farm machinery was damaged including a seed drill which was rotated and a feed wagon which was rolled onto its side. An old barn was toppled. This damage primarily spread from northwest to southeast. Finally, a small outbuilding was tossed back to the north, likely from the strong southerly inflow winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After some early morning severe thunderstorms produced large hail and severe winds, an outflow boundary descended southeast across north central and northeast South Dakota during the evening producing more severe thunderstorms for the region. Severe wind gusts up to near 100 mph, large hail up to golf balls, along with an EF2 tornado brought significant damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant damage occurred to the Long Lake Hutterite Colony. Witnesses said two rounds of wind caused damage to the property. Southerly storm inflow winds ripped multiple roof panels off four separate turkey barns. Debris was spread to the west northwest. Additional damage was done to a feed mill and a machine shed was completely destroyed. Debris from these buildings was tossed from north northwest to south southeast. Other grain bin damage also occurred.  The total path length was about a half mile or slightly more.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant early spring storm wrapped up while moving from Kansas on the morning of Saturday, March 28 to Wisconsin early morning March 29. While temperatures were, in general, too warm for snowfall with the exception of some higher elevation locations receiving 1 to 3 inches of snowfall during the afternoon and evening of March 28, there was appreciable rainfall from 1 to 2 inches from far eastern South Dakota into parts of northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With rainfall which averaged 1.00 to 1.50 inches around the Sioux Falls area, the Big Sioux River at Brandon (gage site BBSS2) returned to flood late month. The river crested at 0.66 feet above flood stage on March 29, resulting in extensive flooding of agricultural land near the river and flooding the north access road to the Big Sioux Recreation Area. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: With rainfall of 1.55 inches 14 miles north northeast of Sioux Falls, 1.13 inches 7 miles west of Garretson and 0.85 inches 4 miles northwest of Renner, the Big Sioux River at Sioux Falls I-90 returned to flood late month.  The river crested 0.62 feet above flood stage on March 31, resulting in flooding of agricultural lands near the river north of Sioux Falls.  The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With one to four inches of liquid residing in the snowpack near and north of Interstate 90 at the start of the month, a more or less ideal melting of the winter snowpack in early March combined with high base flows and wet antecedent conditions to produce minor to major flooding along middle portions of the James River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River at Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) began the month in minor flood, then crested on March 13 in moderate flood, 4.86 feet above flood stage. Impacts were generally limited to flooding of agricultural lands near the river, but flood waters did cause the closing of Shanard Road, east of Mitchell. The river remained at moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

The storm brought a swath of very large hail and strong wind gusts from south of Spearfish, to the Rapid City area, and across portions of the west central and southwestern South Dakota plains. The storm produced two tornadoes; one across the northern Black Hills and the other over western Bennett County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado tracked across forested terrain of the northern Black Hills for 15.5 miles. Many trees were snapped or uprooted along its path. Although the tornado itself did not cause property damage, trees were blown down onto houses and power lines.

The storm produced two tornadoes; one across the northern Black Hills and the other over western Bennett County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado tracked southeast across areas western Bennett County west and south of Swett. A mobile home was destroyed as it rolled 50 feet from its previous location. A few other homes on the edge of the tornado path were also damaged. Some large trees and branches were downed.

Storms tracked across north central and northeast South Dakota during the evening hours of July 24th and into July 25th. Developing in a very moisture rich atmosphere, the storms were able to produce very heavy rain in a short amount of time, especially over parts of northeastern McPherson County, northern Brown County and far northwestern Marshall County. Rainfall amounts ranged from 4 to 8 inches, while localized amounts approached a foot. The heavy rain led to significant overland flooding as well as flooding on Elm Lake and subsequent flooding downstream along the Elm River. Many roads were underwater or completely washed out. There were reports of boats and docks submerged with others freely floating on Elm Lake. The river gauge on the Elm River, just below Elm Lake, (ERFS2) reached a record stage of 19.56 feet in the afternoon of July 25th. The Elm River at Westport (WESS2) crested at a major flood stage of 20.45 feet in the early morning of the 27th.||Some of the heaviest rainfall amounts include, 4.94 inches 13 miles northwest of Britton; 6.75 inches 4 miles northwest of Elm Lake, 6.86 inches 2 miles east northeast of Spring Creek Colony; 7.04 inches 9 miles east of Spring Creek Colony; 7.80 inches 10 miles northwest Frederick; and 11.92 inches 11.5 miles west-northwest of Frederick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many county roads were underwater in northeast Mcpherson county due to flash flooding from very heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed along a cold front during the evening hours. With up to 3000 J/kg MLCAPE but with relatively weak deep-layer shear, storm intensity was sporadic with only a single report of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported to vary between pea and quarter size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front during the late afternoon and early evening hours slashed into an unstable airmass with 2000-3000 J/kg MLCAPE and 30-35 knots of 0-6 km shear. Scattered thunderstorms developed with the thick CAPE profile supportive of large hail and over 1000 J/kg DCAPE resulting in occasional damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Deputy came across deep water flowing across the road.  The full depth was unknown, but it was deep enough to force the deputy to turn around.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak convection developing along a pre-existing boundary led to brief spin-up of a high-based funnel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports were received from spotters and law enforcement of non-thunderstorm funnel clouds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal zone moved into southeast South Dakota during the evening. Scattered stronger storms developed post-frontal in an elevated CAPE environment up to 3000 J/kg, but with weak mid-level flow and deep-layer shear.

A strong jet streak moved across Montana and North Dakota during the afternoon and early evening, providing enough lift to overcome a lid of warmer temperatures aloft across central South Dakota. Storms progressed east and southeast and utilized drying within the elevated layer to generate enough cooling to produce hail, and environmental deep-layer shear of 45 to 55 knots to organize into short bowing segments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SDSU Mesonet station OAKS2 (Oak Lake) measured a wind gust to 83 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Diurnal destabilization produced a narrow corridor of 600-1000 J/kg MLCAPE across south central South Dakota by late afternoon on May 5.  A reinforcing trough in northwest flow dropped southeast across the area in the late afternoon. Despite fairly weak deep-layer shear, an isolated storm briefly produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damages are insured losses. Information is provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant early spring storm wrapped up while moving from Kansas on the morning of Saturday, March 28 to Wisconsin early morning March 29. While temperatures were, in general, too warm for snowfall with the exception of some higher elevation locations receiving 1 to 3 inches of snowfall during the afternoon and evening of March 28, there was appreciable rainfall from 1 to 2 inches from far eastern South Dakota into parts of northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With rainfall of 0.91 inches 2 miles south southeast of Astoria and 0.43 inches near White, the Big Sioux River near Estelline (gage site BSES2) returned to flood at the end of the month. The river crested 0.38 feet above flood stage on March 29, flooding agricultural land near the river. Minor flooding continued at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant early spring storm wrapped up while moving from Kansas on the morning of Saturday, March 28 to Wisconsin early morning March 29. While temperatures were, in general, too warm for snowfall with the exception of some higher elevation locations receiving 1 to 3 inches of snowfall during the afternoon and evening of March 28, there was appreciable rainfall from 1 to 2 inches from far eastern South Dakota into parts of northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With rainfall of 1.58 inches at Rock Valley, 1.51 inches at Canton and an average of 1.00 to 1.50 inches around Sioux Falls, the Big Sioux River above Hawarden (gage site HWDI4) returned to flood at the end of the month. The river crested at 2.52 feet above flood stage on March 30, resulting in significant flooding of crop and pasture land. The river remained at minor flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified moving from southeast Colorado on the morning of March 19, into the Great Lakes early on March 20. A strong band of mid-level frontogenesis progressed slowly eastward across the area during the afternoon and evening hours. The system was initially quite warm, resulting in a swath of moderate to heavy rainfall from northeast Nebraska into northwest Iowa. As the system strengthened on the 19th, much colder air was dragged into the frontal band, and precipitation turned quickly from rain to snow from northwest to southeast, and was accompanied by winds gusting 40 to 55 mph. Snowfall was generally from 1 to 5 inches near and south of Interstate 90, with heaviest bands near the Missouri River and again from the Sioux Falls area eastward along Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed quickly to snowfall late afternoon on March 19, with visibility to around one-half mile at times between 1700-2100CST. The intense snowfall rate made travel hazardous during the late afternoon and early evening as winds increased to a peak of 47 mph 3 miles south of Beresford. Snowfall totaled 2.0 inches 6 miles north northeast of Elk Point.  Around 0735CST on March 20, a northbound bus attempted to change lanes on snow-covered Interstate 29 5 miles south of Elk Point, lost control, and overturned in the east ditch. 11 passengers were (indirectly) injured.

After some early morning severe thunderstorms produced large hail and severe winds, an outflow boundary descended southeast across north central and northeast South Dakota during the evening producing more severe thunderstorms for the region. Severe wind gusts up to near 100 mph, large hail up to golf balls, along with an EF2 tornado brought significant damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down 3 miles north northwest of Wetonka causing significant damage to the Grassland Hutterite Colony.  A large machine shop lost the roof and wall. A large, empty, anchored grain bin was completely removed from its base and the adjacent feed mill was significantly damaged. A 400 foot by 80 foot turkey barn was completely destroyed along with a smaller outbuilding. Debris from these two buildings was scattered in many directions.  A trailer was flipped, freight storage unit rotated and two other outbuildings had complete loss of roof panels. Roof and siding damage occurred to many of the residential buildings. Tree and crop damage had also occurred. The tornado tracked over 2 miles southeast, crossing Mcpherson County Highway 23 and ending about one mile north northeast of Wetonka. Debris from the Grassland Colony was dispersed along the entire track of the tornado.

Unusual warmth with a week of temperatures mainly in the 70s (even 50s and 60s on November 9 less than a day before this storm) was brought to an abrupt end as a deep trough of low pressure moved out of Rockies and into the Plains. An intense lower- to mid-level frontal zone oozed eastward as a strong jet lifted out across the Plains on November 10, initiating widespread precipitation. More rapid shallow cooling occurred across northwest Iowa and northeast Nebraska, and led to a swath of freezing rain which accumulated from a tenth to a half inch during the overnight and morning hours, before changing to snow in the afternoon. Heavier and more persistent snowfall occurred with the deeper cooling to the northwest across southeast South Dakota, far northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, where amounts ranged from 3 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall started briefly as light freezing rain and sleet, with ice accumulation of less than a tenth of an inch during the early morning of November 10.  Snow was briefly heavy between 1100-1400. Total snowfall accumulation reached 6.2 inches 6 miles north northeast of Elk Point.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall, briefly mixed with sleet or freezing drizzle during the morning, became heavy at times between 1100-1500CST.  Accumulations reached 6.7 inches at Vermillion and 6.0 inches 6 miles southeast of Centerville.
