EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in western South Dakota during the evening of the 27th before traveling east across all of South Dakota into the early morning hours on the 28th. As the storms became more organized they produced strong straight-line winds as well as brief tornados. Numerous reports of straight-line winds over 70 mph were documented along with a peak wind gust of 104 mph. Six tornados also occurred in total. The severe winds along with the tornados damaged trees, buildings, campers, and crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down on the east side of Redfield in the early morning hours. First indications of damage occurred in a corn field west of 386th avenue. Damage was then noted on east 8th avenue where several vehicles had windows damaged and a trailer was flipped into a corn field across the street. Trees along the property line with Farmers Meat Processing were damaged, one tree having landed facing west. Farmers Meat Processing signage was also torn out of the ground. The tornado path became clearly evident in the corn field across east 12th avenue. The tornado then traveled northeast into the Blume Seed Building which lost the west side doors and suffered significant wall damage, with the north wall destroyed.  Seed silos on the west side of the building also buckled.  The tornado spread debris from the Blume Seed building northeast into the adjacent grass field. A tree on the northeast side of this grass field toppled to the north with tree debris extending across U.S. Highway 212.  The circulation from the tornado persisted through several soybean and corn fields and resulted in tree damage near 387th Avenue.  The final crop damage was noted just south of 172nd Street.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall, heavy at times between 1200-1600CST, accumulated to 7.0 inches on the west side of Sioux Falls, 6.5 inches 2 miles west southwest of Garretson, and 6.0 inches 4 miles northwest of Renner and 6 miles south of Hartford. Poor road conditions at the onset led to numerous accidents, one of which resulted in the (indirect) fatality of a driver and passenger of a pickup truck which lost control and collided with another vehicle before it rolled in the median.  The driver of the other vehicle suffered non-life threatening injuries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado track started 2 miles east of Redfield near South Dakota Highway 212.  The track was evident through multiple corn fields with the corn completely leveled in some spots.  The tornado crossed 172nd street and traveled through a bean field before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area lifted north across the western Dakotas through the day and deepened.  A cold front extending from this surface low swept east across South Dakota into the evening hours. A strong cold front pushing across central and northeast South Dakota brought several severe storms to the region.  Large hail up to 2 inches in diameter occurred along with a fatal tornado in northern Hand county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado initially touched down in the pasture land east of South Dakota Highway 45, roughly 0.8 miles south of 184th Street.  It continued to track north for roughly 0.6 miles, damaging a tree grove and fence line.  The track then turned west northwest and crossed South Dakota Highway 45, roughly 0.2 miles south of 184th street.  A southbound RV pulling an enclosed trailer was struck. The trailer detached from the RV and the RV was lofted and rolled roughly 200 yards into a corn field on the west side of of South Dakota Highway 45.  The driver was pronounced dead at the scene.  The tornado track continued west northwest through the corn field for an estimated 0.4 miles.  The tornado was at its peak width and intensity at this point.  Corn was completely flattened  in some areas with a distinct convergent pattern.  As the tornado weakened and roped out the path took a southwest turn and persisted for roughly another 0.6 miles before coming to an end in the corn field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The flooding on the James River at Columbia finally fell below flood stage at the end of August while the flooding continued at Stratford into September. The James River at Columbia had been above flood stage since April 2nd of 2019 for a total of 518 days or for  17 months. The previous record of continuous days above flood stage was 266 days in 2011.

Unusual warmth with a week of temperatures mainly in the 70s (even 50s and 60s on November 9 less than a day before this storm) was brought to an abrupt end as a deep trough of low pressure moved out of Rockies and into the Plains. An intense lower- to mid-level frontal zone oozed eastward as a strong jet lifted out across the Plains on November 10, initiating widespread precipitation. More rapid shallow cooling occurred across northwest Iowa and northeast Nebraska, and led to a swath of freezing rain which accumulated from a tenth to a half inch during the overnight and morning hours, before changing to snow in the afternoon. Heavier and more persistent snowfall occurred with the deeper cooling to the northwest across southeast South Dakota, far northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, where amounts ranged from 3 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall, heavy at times between 1200-1600CST, accumulated to 6.5 inches 5 miles northeast of Salem and 5.5 inches 6 miles northwest of Montrose.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in western South Dakota during the evening of the 27th before traveling east across all of South Dakota into the early morning hours on the 28th. As the storms became more organized they produced strong straight-line winds as well as brief tornados. Numerous reports of straight-line winds over 70 mph were documented along with a peak wind gust of 104 mph. Six tornados also occurred in total. The severe winds along with the tornados damaged trees, buildings, campers, and crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated seventy-five mph winds rolled and destroyed a trailer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a weak convergence axis across central South Dakota in the late afternoon hours. Slow moving storms made use of up to 3000 J/kg MLCAPE and marginal deep-layer shear around 30 knots to produce considerable large hail and spotty damaging winds through early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust of 65 mph was measured by a personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developing along a nearly stationary frontal boundary through central South Dakota and Nebraska organized into a line by late evening.  The environment with extreme mid-level instability (greater than 9 degrees C/km) and 1200-1500 J/kg DCAPE helped to enhance damaging wind gusts across southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Dakota RWIS site SD554 measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lift associated with a jet streak across southern North Dakota interacted with an unstable and weakly capped environment with 30 to 40 knots of deep-layer shear to produce a couple of smaller clusters of thunderstorms with large hail and damaging wind during the evening and overnight which progressed east and southeast across southeast South Dakota.  The final cluster was a larger-scale bowing segment which produced damaging wind gusts across south central South Dakota.  Repeated storms with heavy rainfall resulted in spotty flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail damaged several cars. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent environment with 2500-3000 J/kg MLCAPE remained weakly capped until a wave moved across northern Nebraska by mid to late evening.  Clusters of thunderstorms developed and organized into short bowing segments, which produced spotty damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was mostly pea-sized with some up to the size of a penny, which was accompanied by winds to around 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent environment with 2500-3000 J/kg MLCAPE remained weakly capped until a wave moved across northern Nebraska by mid to late evening.  Clusters of thunderstorms developed and organized into short bowing segments, which produced spotty damaging wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued dry conditions allowed the James River to fall below flood stage after numerous months of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River at Huron (gage site HURS2) began the month at 0.1 feet above flood stage, and fell below flood stage on August 2.  Minor flooding of agricultural areas was the main impact.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developing along a nearly stationary frontal boundary through central South Dakota and Nebraska organized into a line by late evening.  The environment with extreme mid-level instability (greater than 9 degrees C/km) and 1200-1500 J/kg DCAPE helped to enhance damaging wind gusts across southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gust to 65 mph measured by hand-held anemometer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms developed along a cold front approaching Interstate 29 in the late afternoon, with MLCAPE of 1500-2000 J/kg.  Storms quickly become outflow dominant and produced damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds downed a few 3 inch diameter tree limbs. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lift associated with a jet streak across southern North Dakota interacted with an unstable and weakly capped environment with 30 to 40 knots of deep-layer shear to produce a couple of smaller clusters of thunderstorms with large hail and damaging wind during the evening and overnight which progressed east and southeast across southeast South Dakota.  The final cluster was a larger-scale bowing segment which produced damaging wind gusts across south central South Dakota.  Repeated storms with heavy rainfall resulted in spotty flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Dakota RWIS station SD554 14 miles west of Platte near the Platte-Winner bridge measured a peak wind gust of 72 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued runoff from snow melt as well as early Spring precipitation of 1 to 2 inches in the northern basin kept the slow-draining James River Basin in flood.

An early month crest of 2.92 feet above flood stage occurred on April 2. Numerous county and township roads near the gage were inundated. The river remained at moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher flows from rainfall and snow melt were sustained by heavy rainfall again at the end of March and beginning of April.  A drier period heading into mid-month allowed the river to fall below flood stage.

Then, after dropping briefly below flood stage on April 3, the river rose back above flood stage on April 6 and crested 0.09 feet above flood stage on April 7.  Impacts were limited to significant flooding of crop and pasture land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The long term and record continuous days of flooding on the James River at Stratford finally came to an end by the end of September.  The James River at Stratford was above flood stage for a year and a half from early April 2019 to late September 2020.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across the region, bringing rain to the area initially. Unusually cold air moved in with the system and allowed rain to turn over to snow during the nighttime hours across elevations mostly above 3500 feet, and especially above 4500 feet. The heaviest snow was at or above 5000 feet elevation, where five to ten inches fell in many areas, with higher amounts at the highest elevations of the Black Hills. The heavy, wet snow downed trees, branches, and power lines, causing scattered power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across the region, bringing rain to the area initially. Unusually cold air moved in with the system and allowed rain to turn over to snow during the nighttime hours across elevations mostly above 3500 feet, and especially above 4500 feet. The heaviest snow was at or above 5000 feet elevation, where five to ten inches fell in many areas, with higher amounts at the highest elevations of the Black Hills. The heavy, wet snow downed trees, branches, and power lines, causing scattered power outages.

A cold front pushing across central South Dakota and Nebraska ignited severe storms overnight. With elevated instability of 1000-1500 J/kg and an increasing low-level jet, numerous storms formed along the 700 hPa baroclinic zone with a few taking on bowing characteristics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated weather station KAGZ near Wagner measured a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

Other than brief rises resulting from one to two inches of rainfall in middle and upper portions of the James River Basin, a downward trend to James River levels continued during July 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from June, the James River at Mitchell (gage site JRMS2) started the month at moderate flood 3.23 feet above flood stage. After falling river levels early in the month, 1.36 inches of rainfall near Forestburg and 0.71 inches at Mitchell caused a secondary peak 1.43 feet above flood stage on July 16. Several county and township roads near the river remained under water at the start of the month, with inundation of agricultural land the primary threat thereafter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Other than brief rises resulting from one to two inches of rainfall in middle and upper portions of the James River Basin, a downward trend to James River levels continued during July 2020.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a result of downstream routing of floodwaters and rainfall the first week of the month totaling 2.10 inches at Parkston and 2.12 inches 7 miles south of Parkston, the James River near Scotland (gage site SCOS2) returned to minor flood and crested 0.69 feet above flood stage on July 4. Significant flooding occurred to agricultural land near the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued runoff from snow melt as well as early Spring precipitation of 1 to 2 inches in the northern basin kept the slow-draining James River Basin in flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of flooding from March, the James River near Yankton (gage site YNNS2) began the month at a peak 3.49 feet above flood stage on April 1, then fell gradually below flood stage on April 20. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large-scale bow echo fueled by MLCAPE around 2500 J/kg and 30 to 35 knots of deep-layer shear developed across central South Dakota, before weakening toward the James River where shear profiles were much weaker around 20 knots.  Storms produced damaging wind gusts prior to weakening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An anchored play set and gazebo were destroyed. Several large limbs were also downed by the strong winds.  Minor damage occurred to house skirting and siding. Property damage amounts are rough estimates. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developing along a nearly stationary frontal boundary through central South Dakota and Nebraska organized into a line by late evening.  The environment with extreme mid-level instability (greater than 9 degrees C/km) and 1200-1500 J/kg DCAPE helped to enhance damaging wind gusts across southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Dakota RWIS gage SD571 measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong frontal forcing resulted in the redevelopment of elevated thunderstorms during the very early morning to the west of the band from the previous evening. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A confluent trough developed across the Northern Plains and locked in the cold air which had pushed southward and produced numerous record low maximum and minimum temperatures.  As an extended period of warm advection and frontogenesis intensified during the afternoon of October 24, a broad area of snowfall developed, with periods of moderate to briefly heavy snowfall during the morning and early afternoon of October 25. Numerous snowfall records were established on October 24th and 25th to go along with the record cold temperatures, which also propelled the month into the top 3 snowiest Octobers at Brookings, Flandreau, Marshall, Minneota, Tracy and Yankton (1st), Windom and De Smet (2nd), and Sibley, Sanborn and Pipestone (3rd).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.0 inches at Woonsocket and 3.0 inches 4 miles north northeast of Forestburg.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 5.5 inches at Vermillion and 4.0 inches 6 miles southeast of Centerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms fired along a slowly moving cold front during the early evening. Initially discrete, strong updrafts with 2000-2500 J/kg MLCAPE and deep-layer shear around 50 knots resulted in supercells, which later congealed into a slowly progressing line.  With most storms forming slightly elevated behind the boundary, most severe weather was in the form of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A confluent trough developed across the Northern Plains and locked in the cold air which had pushed southward and produced numerous record low maximum and minimum temperatures.  As an extended period of warm advection and frontogenesis intensified during the afternoon of October 24, a broad area of snowfall developed, with periods of moderate to briefly heavy snowfall during the morning and early afternoon of October 25. Numerous snowfall records were established on October 24th and 25th to go along with the record cold temperatures, which also propelled the month into the top 3 snowiest Octobers at Brookings, Flandreau, Marshall, Minneota, Tracy and Yankton (1st), Windom and De Smet (2nd), and Sibley, Sanborn and Pipestone (3rd).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 5.5 inches at Colton, 4.3 inches at Sioux Falls and 4.1 inches near Garretson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued warm air advection and the advancement of steep mid-level lapse rates brought MLCAPE up to 2000 J/kg.  Storms evolved into small bowing segments with deep-layer shear up to 50 knots and DCAPE values over 1000 J/kg while moving from south central to east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust was measured by a personal weather station. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong frontal forcing resulted in the redevelopment of elevated thunderstorms during the very early morning to the west of the band from the previous evening.  Storms quickly became linear with the development of a couple of mesovortices, which were the focus of damaging winds while tracking northeast along the line.  Heavy training rainfall brought areas of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power poles were downed or snapped by wind. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

Some tree limbs were downed and minor power outages reported.

With one to four inches of liquid residing in the snowpack near and north of Interstate 90 at the start of the month, a more or less ideal melting of the winter snowpack in early March produced minor flooding along middle portions of the Vermillion River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Vermillion River near Davis (gage site DVSS2) crested 1.20 feet above flood stage on March 7. Water encroached on U.S. Highway 18 west of Davis, with large amounts of agricultural land flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy rainfall returned high water levels along the James River. Monthly rainfall was above normal (4 to 6 inches) in the middle and upper portions of the river basin, with a concentrated rainfall from 2 to 3 inches around June 8-9.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After mid-month rainfall of 2 to 3 inches in the mid-James River Basin, the James River near Scotland (gage site SCOS2) rose above flood stage, with a peak at 0.90 feet above flood stage on June 14.  Greatest impact was flooding of agricultural land near the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With one to four inches of liquid residing in the snowpack near and north of Interstate 90 at the start of the month, a more or less ideal melting of the winter snowpack in early March produced minor flooding along much of the upper and middle portions of the Big Sioux River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River at Sioux Falls I-90 (gage site SFLS2) rose above flood stage on March 5, and crested 1.8 feet above flood stage on March 15. Impacts were primarily inundation of lower-lying agricultural lands north of Sioux Falls.

With cold air entrenched north of a warm front across southern Nebraska and Iowa, lift associated with a strong upper-level jet focused atop the cold dome resulted in widespread light freezing rain and drizzle, and some areas of snow deeper into the cold air.  The freezing rain developed during the late night and early morning hours, and generally ended by later morning or early afternoon. Ice accumulations varied from a few hundredths to almost a quarter inch, while accumulated snow was mainly less than an inch from west of the James River through the Highway 14 corridor in east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing drizzle started prior to sunrise and continued through the morning. Ice accumulation on elevated surfaces reached upward of one tenth of an inch, with lesser ice also accumulating on roads and bridges which produced dangerous conditions for several hours. Drizzle changed to light snow around 1400CST, with accumulation of 0.2 inch 4 miles northeast of Forestburg on top of the light coating of ice.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing drizzle started prior to sunrise and continued through the morning. Ice accumulation on elevated surfaces reached upward of one tenth of an inch, with lesser ice also accumulating on roads and bridges which produced dangerous conditions for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of three rare October heavy snow events to the affect the region occurred on October 20th. An upper level low pressure trough coming in from the northwest riding over abnormally cold surface air brought heavy snow from 6 to 13 inches across northeast South Dakota. The snowfall was very heavy at times enhanced by thunderstorms. Some snowfall amounts include, 6 inches 4 miles west of Sisseton, Doland, 4 miles north of Victor and 2 miles east of Watertown; 7 inches at Webster, Summit, and 2 miles northwest of Westport; 8 inches at Milbank; and 13 inches at Wilmot.||The three rare heavy snow producing systems along with the abnormally cold air set numerous temperature and snowfall records.  Most locations across the region had both their top ten coldest and snowiest Octobers on record. In fact, Mobridge, Sisseton, and Timber Lake had their snowiest October on record.  There were also numerous daily temperature records set for many locations from the 19th through the 28th.

A strong surface low pressure area in central South Dakota along a stalled southwest to northeast surface boundary brought numerous severe thunderstorms to central and northeast South Dakota. The severe storms moved over the same areas multiple times into the early morning hours of June 8th. Large hail from quarter to golf ball size, wind gusts of 60 to 90 mph, along with four tornadoes occurred.  Numerous structures and trees were damaged or destroyed from these storms.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: West of South Dakota Highway 47, minor roof damage occurred to a house, a calving shed was pushed 10 feet to the northeast and sheet metal from a barn was tossed across the highway to the east northeast into an open field. On the main property, damage was done to a dog kennel, a tree was uprooted, pine trees were snapped at the trunk and stripped of needles, fencing was leveled, and a batting cage and playhouse were tossed. Further northeast several calf sheds were moved or rolled. One calving shed was lofted into the air over a grove of trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low pressure area in central South Dakota along a stalled southwest to northeast surface boundary brought numerous severe thunderstorms to central and northeast South Dakota. The severe storms moved over the same areas multiple times into the early morning hours of June 8th. Large hail from quarter to golf ball size, wind gusts of 60 to 90 mph, along with four tornadoes occurred.  Numerous structures and trees were damaged or destroyed from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pole barn was completely destroyed with anchoring poles pulled straight up out of the ground. A second well anchored outbuilding was ripped off of its foundation while a third outbuilding was tossed. Debris from all three destroyed buildings was spread between one half to three quarters of a mile to the northeast across an open field. Debris lumber from the buildings was pierced into the ground. Some fencing was damaged and farm equipment was pushed and tipped over. The main house suffered shingle and siding damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low pressure area in central South Dakota along a stalled southwest to northeast surface boundary brought numerous severe thunderstorms to central and northeast South Dakota. The severe storms moved over the same areas multiple times into the early morning hours of June 8th. Large hail from quarter to golf ball size, wind gusts of 60 to 90 mph, along with four tornadoes occurred.  Numerous structures and trees were damaged or destroyed from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado damage started with tree branches tossed about a quarter mile across an open pasture. The next damage point was an uprooted tree as well as two calving sheds completely destroyed. Sheet metal and lumber from the sheds were tossed about a quarter mile across an open pasture. The last evidence of the tornado was a narrow path of downed trees in the middle of a line of trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second of three rare October heavy snow events occurred across north central and northeast South Dakota on October 21st and 22nd. An upper level low pressure trough from the northwest traversing over rare October cold air brought heavy snow of 6 to 13 inches to the region.  Roads became snow covered and difficult to travel.  Also, school starts were delayed or were closed for the day.  ||Snowfall amounts from across the region include, 6 inches 4 miles northwest of Onida, Rosholt, and 6 miles west of Pierpont; 7 inches at Roscoe, Aberdeen, and Timber Lake; 8 inches 4 miles north of Victor and 12 miles north of Highmore; 9 inches 4 miles west of Sisseton, Selby, and 7 miles east of Hayes; 10 inches 18 miles north of Holabird and Britton; and 11 inches 11 miles southwest of Eureka.  Locations with a foot or more of snow include, 12 inches 5 miles northwest of Leola, 2 miles southeast of Kenel, and 4 miles southeast of Herreid; and 13 inches 1 mile south of Artas.||The three rare heavy snow producing systems along with the abnormally cold air set numerous temperature and snowfall records.  Most locations across the region had both their top ten coldest and snowiest Octobers on record. In fact, Mobridge, Sisseton, and Timber Lake had their snowiest October on record.  There were also numerous daily temperature records set for many locations from the 19th through the 28th.
Downslope winds gusted to over 60 mph along the Sisseton Hills.

Dissipating showers over parts of central and northeast South Dakota early in the morning produced heat bursts and high winds. Heat bursts produce sudden increases in surface temperatures and winds along with a decrease in surface humidity. High wind gusts ranged from around 65 to over 80 mph causing some tree and structural damage.  The highest wind gust with the heat bursts was 82 mph in Clark. Many other locations recorded strong wind gusts of 50 to 55 mph from heat bursts. Temperatures rose anywhere from 5 to 10 degrees from heat bursts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving disturbance spread a swath of warm advection precipitation across South Dakota into southwest Minnesota during the early morning hours of October 20, 2020.  While temperatures were initially warm enough to support rain, a combination of wet bulb cooling and the diabatic impact of intense lift in the mid-level frontogenetic zone caused a rapid and persistent change over to snow. A band of 3 to 7 inches of snowfall resulted from east central South Dakota near Interstate 29 eastward along the Buffalo Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.0 inches at De Smet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With cold air entrenched north of a warm front across southern Nebraska and Iowa, lift associated with a strong upper-level jet focused atop the cold dome resulted in widespread light freezing rain and drizzle, and some areas of snow deeper into the cold air.  The freezing rain developed during the late night and early morning hours, and generally ended by later morning or early afternoon. Ice accumulations varied from a few hundredths to almost a quarter inch, while accumulated snow was mainly less than an inch from west of the James River through the Highway 14 corridor in east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing drizzle started prior to sunrise and continued through the morning. Ice accumulation on elevated surfaces reached upward of two tenths of an inch, with lesser ice also accumulating on roads and bridges which produced dangerous conditions for several hours.  Brief power outages occurred during the early morning hours.

A strong storm system moved across the region, bringing significant early season snowfall to much of the area. The heaviest snow fell in several areas, including one band across portions of Perkins, Ziebach, and Haakon Counties, another across the northern Black Hills and adjacent foothills, and also across parts of far southern South Dakota. Snowfall in these areas were mostly in the five to ten inch range, with locally higher amounts across the northern Black Hills. Elsewhere, three to seven inches of snow were reported.  Unseasonably cold air was also in place, especially behind the system, with record or near record temperatures across the area.

A strong surface low pressure area in central South Dakota along a stalled southwest to northeast surface boundary brought numerous severe thunderstorms to central and northeast South Dakota. The severe storms moved over the same areas multiple times into the early morning hours of June 8th. Large hail from quarter to golf ball size, wind gusts of 60 to 90 mph, along with four tornadoes occurred.  Numerous structures and trees were damaged or destroyed from these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An irrigation pivot was flipped over by estimated seventy mph winds.

A strong storm system moved across the region, bringing significant early season snowfall to much of the area. The heaviest snow fell in several areas, including one band across portions of Perkins, Ziebach, and Haakon Counties, another across the northern Black Hills and adjacent foothills, and also across parts of far southern South Dakota. Snowfall in these areas were mostly in the five to ten inch range, with locally higher amounts across the northern Black Hills. Elsewhere, three to seven inches of snow were reported.  Unseasonably cold air was also in place, especially behind the system, with record or near record temperatures across the area.

With cold air entrenched north of a warm front across southern Nebraska and Iowa, lift associated with a strong upper-level jet focused atop the cold dome resulted in widespread light freezing rain and drizzle, and some areas of snow deeper into the cold air.  The freezing rain developed during the late night and early morning hours, and generally ended by later morning or early afternoon. Ice accumulations varied from a few hundredths to almost a quarter inch, while accumulated snow was mainly less than an inch from west of the James River through the Highway 14 corridor in east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing drizzle started prior to sunrise and continued into the early afternoon before changing to light snowfall.  Light ice accumulation on roadways produced treacherous conditions on Interstate 90 for several hours.  Ice accumulation was around a tenth of an inch, topped by 0.7 inch of snowfall at White Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last in three rare October heavy snow events affected mainly central and north central South Dakota from October 24th into the 25th. The final upper level low pressure trough from the northwest riding over the much below normal cold air at the surface brought the last round of heavy snow accumulating to 6 to 11 inches.  Some snowfall amounts include, 6 inches at Murdo; 7 inches 4 miles northeast of Reliance and Timber Lake; 8 inches at Eagle Butte, 3 miles southwest of Whitlocks Bay, and 6 miles north of Shambo Ranch; 10 inches 3 miles south of West Bend Recreation Area and 1 mile west of Bunker; with 11 inches 8 miles northwest of Okobojo Creek Recreation area.||The three rare heavy snow producing systems along with the abnormally cold air set numerous temperature and snowfall records.  Most locations across the region had both their top ten coldest and snowiest Octobers on record. In fact, Mobridge, Sisseton, and Timber Lake had their snowiest October on record.  There were also numerous daily temperature records set for many locations from the 19th through the 28th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain and copious amounts of hail combined to produce street flooding in Custer. The excessive runoff overwhelmed culverts and caused flooding along French Creek upstream of Stockade Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation continued well-below normal across southeast South Dakota, varying from 25 to 50 percent of normal. Temperatures were well-below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate (D1) drought evolved to severe (D2) drought conditions early in the month due to very warm and dry conditions. Monthly precipitation at Menno was less than one-third of normal, but the average temperature finished the month around 6 degrees below normal which limited the advancing drought conditions. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across the region, bringing significant early season snowfall to much of the area. The heaviest snow fell in several areas, including one band across portions of Perkins, Ziebach, and Haakon Counties, another across the northern Black Hills and adjacent foothills, and also across parts of far southern South Dakota. Snowfall in these areas were mostly in the five to ten inch range, with locally higher amounts across the northern Black Hills. Elsewhere, three to seven inches of snow were reported.  Unseasonably cold air was also in place, especially behind the system, with record or near record temperatures across the area.

With cold air entrenched north of a warm front across southern Nebraska and Iowa, lift associated with a strong upper-level jet focused atop the cold dome resulted in widespread light freezing rain and drizzle, and some areas of snow deeper into the cold air.  The freezing rain developed during the late night and early morning hours, and generally ended by later morning or early afternoon. Ice accumulations varied from a few hundredths to almost a quarter inch, while accumulated snow was mainly less than an inch from west of the James River through the Highway 14 corridor in east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing drizzle started prior to sunrise and continued through the morning. Ice accumulation on elevated surfaces reached upward of a tenth of an inch, with lesser ice also accumulating on roads and bridges which produced dangerous conditions for several hours.  Brief power outages to over 100 customers occurred during the early morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last in three rare October heavy snow events affected mainly central and north central South Dakota from October 24th into the 25th. The final upper level low pressure trough from the northwest riding over the much below normal cold air at the surface brought the last round of heavy snow accumulating to 6 to 11 inches.  Some snowfall amounts include, 6 inches at Murdo; 7 inches 4 miles northeast of Reliance and Timber Lake; 8 inches at Eagle Butte, 3 miles southwest of Whitlocks Bay, and 6 miles north of Shambo Ranch; 10 inches 3 miles south of West Bend Recreation Area and 1 mile west of Bunker; with 11 inches 8 miles northwest of Okobojo Creek Recreation area.||The three rare heavy snow producing systems along with the abnormally cold air set numerous temperature and snowfall records.  Most locations across the region had both their top ten coldest and snowiest Octobers on record. In fact, Mobridge, Sisseton, and Timber Lake had their snowiest October on record.  There were also numerous daily temperature records set for many locations from the 19th through the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low pressure area in central South Dakota along a stalled southwest to northeast surface boundary brought numerous severe thunderstorms to central and northeast South Dakota. The severe storms moved over the same areas multiple times into the early morning hours of June 8th. Large hail from quarter to golf ball size, wind gusts of 60 to 90 mph, along with four tornadoes occurred.  Numerous structures and trees were damaged or destroyed from these storms.

Unusual warmth with a week of temperatures mainly in the 70s (even 50s and 60s on November 9 less than a day before this storm) was brought to an abrupt end as a deep trough of low pressure moved out of Rockies and into the Plains. An intense lower- to mid-level frontal zone oozed eastward as a strong jet lifted out across the Plains on November 10, initiating widespread precipitation. More rapid shallow cooling occurred across northwest Iowa and northeast Nebraska, and led to a swath of freezing rain which accumulated from a tenth to a half inch during the overnight and morning hours, before changing to snow in the afternoon. Heavier and more persistent snowfall occurred with the deeper cooling to the northwest across southeast South Dakota, far northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, where amounts ranged from 3 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.5 inches at Alexandria.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 5.4 inches 2 miles north of Chester and 4.0 inches at Madison.

Unusual warmth with a week of temperatures mainly in the 70s (even 50s and 60s on November 9 less than a day before this storm) was brought to an abrupt end as a deep trough of low pressure moved out of Rockies and into the Plains. An intense lower- to mid-level frontal zone oozed eastward as a strong jet lifted out across the Plains on November 10, initiating widespread precipitation. More rapid shallow cooling occurred across northwest Iowa and northeast Nebraska, and led to a swath of freezing rain which accumulated from a tenth to a half inch during the overnight and morning hours, before changing to snow in the afternoon. Heavier and more persistent snowfall occurred with the deeper cooling to the northwest across southeast South Dakota, far northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, where amounts ranged from 3 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 5.5 inches 8 miles west northwest of Springfield and 4.8 inches at Tyndall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived supercell thunderstorm continued its track from northeastern Wyoming, across the northern Black Hills, and into south central South Dakota. The storm left very large hail in its wake, especially from the Spearfish area to the north and east parts of Rapid City.  Wind gusts to 60 mph were also reported. Wind driven large hail produced significant vehicle and property damage in some areas. The storm finally weakened as it moved into Nebraska late in the evening.

Strong southerly winds developed during the morning hours over south central South Dakota ahead of a cold front. Sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph and gusts over 50 mph were recorded over much of the area.

Broad warm advection developed early on December 29 as a strong trough aloft sheared northeast through the central and northern Plains.  A period of strong frontogenesis shifted northeast, accompanied by a deep dendritic layer which produced snowfall rates up to an inch per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.0 inches at Huron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad warm advection developed early on December 29 as a strong trough aloft sheared northeast through the central and northern Plains.  A period of strong frontogenesis shifted northeast, accompanied by a deep dendritic layer which produced snowfall rates up to an inch per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to briefly heavy snowfall at times totaled 6.0 inches 2 miles north of Lake Andes and 4.5 inches at Platte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad warm advection developed early on December 29 as a strong trough aloft sheared northeast through the central and northern Plains.  A period of strong frontogenesis shifted northeast, accompanied by a deep dendritic layer which produced snowfall rates up to an inch per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.0 inches at Wessington Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions with temperatures at record or near-record warm levels on December 22 underwent a drastic change over a brief 24 hour period, as a sharp upper-level trough in westerly flow amplified while translating from the Pacific Northwest to the Plains. Low pressure developed and deepened rapidly while moving from South Dakota around 0000CST December 23 to the northern Great Lakes by 0600CST December 24.||Very steep lower to mid-level lapse rates and lift focused within a 200-250hPa deep layer supportive of effective dendritic growth resulted in intense precipitation banding over a 5 to 8 hour period. Winds in the lower mixed layer reached 40 to 60 knots as Arctic air surged into the area behind the system, producing numerous hours of widespread whiteout conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted as high as 66 mph at Huron, 62 mph near Wolsey and 60 mph near Cavour, and despite a lack of pre-storm snow cover, snowfall which accumulated to 2.9 inches at Huron and 2.3 inches 2 miles southeast of Wessington resulted in widespread whiteout conditions for several hours. Travel was not advised during the blizzard across all of southeast SD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions with temperatures at record or near-record warm levels on December 22 underwent a drastic change over a brief 24 hour period, as a sharp upper-level trough in westerly flow amplified while translating from the Pacific Northwest to the Plains. Low pressure developed and deepened rapidly while moving from South Dakota around 0000CST December 23 to the northern Great Lakes by 0600CST December 24.||Very steep lower to mid-level lapse rates and lift focused within a 200-250hPa deep layer supportive of effective dendritic growth resulted in intense precipitation banding over a 5 to 8 hour period. Winds in the lower mixed layer reached 40 to 60 knots as Arctic air surged into the area behind the system, producing numerous hours of widespread whiteout conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted as high as 63 mph near Dell Rapids and 62 mph at Sioux Falls, and despite a lack of pre-storm snow cover, snowfall which accumulated to 4.5 inches 6 miles south of Hartford, 3.0 inches at Colton and 2.8 inches at Sioux Falls resulted in widespread whiteout conditions for several hours. Travel was not advised during the blizzard across all of southeast SD.  At 1141CST, fifteen vehicles collided in whiteout conditions on northbound Interstate 29 around mile marker 99 which closed the road for 7 hours and resulted in 6 indirect injuries.  At 1252CST, another 30 vehicles crashed on westbound Interstate 90 three miles west of Sioux Falls (mile marker 393) which closed the roadway for 8 hours and resulted in 5 indirect injuries.  Another multi-car collision occurred on Interstate 29 in Sioux Falls, which was just one of several dozen other accidents.  All air traffic was cancelled for December 23 at Joe Foss Field in Sioux Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense surface low pressure area along with a powerful cold front brought snow and high winds to northeast South Dakota on December 23rd. The surface low pressure area and cold front tracked from west to east across the region from the early morning into the afternoon hours. Extremely strong northerly winds, gusting from 50 to 70 mph, combined with the snow created widespread blizzard conditions and hazardous travel.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 5 inches across the region. The highest wind gusts included, 66 mph at Victor and Watertown; 67 mph at Mellette and 7 miles northeast of Estelline; 68 mph at Aberdeen and 3 miles southwest of Big Stone City.||Temperatures also dropped significantly behind the system. In fact, above normal and even record temperatures in the 50s and lower 60s on Tuesday, December 22nd,  dropped 40 to 50 degrees by the late afternoon of Wednesday, December 23rd.||Much of northeast South Dakota was placed under no travel advisories with many reports of traffic accidents and stranded motorists. A woman driving to work from her home in rural Amherst to Bristol became disoriented in the blizzard and drove into a slough and died. She was recovered over a month later on January 29th. Also, a truck carrying 9 tanks of hydrogen sulfide went off the highway 1 mile west of Webster in the blizzard and into a lake. The driver was rescued and was unharmed. A hazmat team was called in to ensure safe retrieval of the truck from the lake with no leaks from the tanks noted.

An intense surface low pressure area and strong cold front brought high winds along with light rain and snow to central, north central, and into part of northeast South Dakota from late on December 22nd into December 23rd. The system tracked from west to east across the region, with the snow and light rain occurring during the overnight/early morning hours. Snowfall amounts of 1 to 5 inches combined with the high winds brought periods of reduced visibility across the region. Warm temperatures in advance of the precipitation led to flash freezing on road surfaces. The flash freezing/icing along with the extremely strong northerly winds gusting to 50 to 70 mph and lowered visibility created hazardous travel conditions.  The highest wind gusts included, 65 mph at Stephan and Reliance; 66 mph 17 miles west of Polo; and 69 mph 10 miles west of Long Lake.  ||The temperatures dropped significantly behind the system. In fact, above normal and even record temperatures ranging from the 50s to low 70s on Tuesday, December 22nd had dropped 40 to 50 degrees by the late afternoon of Wednesday, December 23rd.

An intense surface low pressure area and strong cold front brought high winds along with light rain and snow to central, north central, and into part of northeast South Dakota from late on December 22nd into December 23rd. Snowfall amounts of 1 to 5 inches combined with the high winds brought periods of reduced visibility across the region. Conditions with temperatures at record or near-record warm levels on December 22 underwent a drastic change over a brief 24 hour period, as a sharp upper-level trough in westerly flow amplified while translating from the Pacific Northwest to the Plains. Low pressure developed and deepened rapidly while moving from South Dakota around 0000CST December 23 to the northern Great Lakes by 0600CST December 24.||Very steep lower to mid-level lapse rates and lift focused within a 200-250hPa deep layer supportive of effective dendritic growth resulted in intense precipitation banding over a 5 to 8 hour period. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted as high as 56 mph at Brookings and 52 mph at Volga, and despite a lack of pre-storm snow cover, snowfall which accumulated to 4.5 inches at Brookings and 3.0 inches 6 miles west of Bruce resulted in widespread whiteout conditions for several hours. Travel was not advised during the blizzard across all of southeast SD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense surface low pressure area along with a powerful cold front brought snow and high winds to northeast South Dakota on December 23rd. The surface low pressure area and cold front tracked from west to east across the region from the early morning into the afternoon hours. Extremely strong northerly winds, gusting from 50 to 70 mph, combined with the snow created widespread blizzard conditions and hazardous travel.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 5 inches across the region. The highest wind gusts included, 66 mph at Victor and Watertown; 67 mph at Mellette and 7 miles northeast of Estelline; 68 mph at Aberdeen and 3 miles southwest of Big Stone City.||Temperatures also dropped significantly behind the system. In fact, above normal and even record temperatures in the 50s and lower 60s on Tuesday, December 22nd,  dropped 40 to 50 degrees by the late afternoon of Wednesday, December 23rd.||Much of northeast South Dakota was placed under no travel advisories with many reports of traffic accidents and stranded motorists. A woman driving to work from her home in rural Amherst to Bristol became disoriented in the blizzard and drove into a slough and died. She was recovered over a month later on January 29th. Also, a truck carrying 9 tanks of hydrogen sulfide went off the highway 1 mile west of Webster in the blizzard and into a lake. The driver was rescued and was unharmed. A hazmat team was called in to ensure safe retrieval of the truck from the lake with no leaks from the tanks noted.

Strong south winds, above normal temperatures, along with low relative humidity contributed to a wildfire 4 miles northwest of Altamont. A trailer house being pulled had rolled into the north ditch near the intersection of U.S Highway 212 and 475th avenue in northern Deuel county in the mid morning hours. While crews were recovering the trailer house from the ditch, the trailer house caught fire. The fire then spread to the grass in the ditch and continued to spread because of the strong winds and dead grass. The fire covered an area from U.S. Highway 212 to 168th street between 475th and 474th avenue or approximately 1000 acres.

Travel was not advised during the blizzard across all of southeast SD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense surface low pressure area along with a powerful cold front brought snow and high winds to northeast South Dakota on December 23rd. The surface low pressure area and cold front tracked from west to east across the region from the early morning into the afternoon hours. Extremely strong northerly winds, gusting from 50 to 70 mph, combined with the snow created widespread blizzard conditions and hazardous travel.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 5 inches across the region. The highest wind gusts included, 66 mph at Victor and Watertown; 67 mph at Mellette and 7 miles northeast of Estelline; 68 mph at Aberdeen and 3 miles southwest of Big Stone City.||Temperatures also dropped significantly behind the system. In fact, above normal and even record temperatures in the 50s and lower 60s on Tuesday, December 22nd,  dropped 40 to 50 degrees by the late afternoon of Wednesday, December 23rd.||Much of northeast South Dakota was placed under no travel advisories with many reports of traffic accidents and stranded motorists. A woman driving to work from her home in rural Amherst to Bristol became disoriented in the blizzard and drove into a slough and died. She was recovered over a month later on January 29th. Also, a truck carrying 9 tanks of hydrogen sulfide went off the highway 1 mile west of Webster in the blizzard and into a lake. The driver was rescued and was unharmed. A hazmat team was called in to ensure safe retrieval of the truck from the lake with no leaks from the tanks noted.

Conditions with temperatures at record or near-record warm levels on December 22 underwent a drastic change over a brief 24 hour period, as a sharp upper-level trough in westerly flow amplified while translating from the Pacific Northwest to the Plains. Low pressure developed and deepened rapidly while moving from South Dakota around 0000CST December 23 to the northern Great Lakes by 0600CST December 24.||Very steep lower to mid-level lapse rates and lift focused within a 200-250hPa deep layer supportive of effective dendritic growth resulted in intense precipitation banding over a 5 to 8 hour period. Travel was not advised during the blizzard across all of southeast SD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad warm advection developed early on December 29 as a strong trough aloft sheared northeast through the central and northern Plains.  A period of strong frontogenesis shifted northeast, accompanied by a deep dendritic layer which produced snowfall rates up to an inch per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to briefly heavy snowfall at times totaled 6.8 inches 4 miles south southwest of Pickstown, 6.5 inches 11 miles south southeast of Burke and 6.0 inches at Gregory.
